findings.title: No script aggregates per-task pass/fail into the headline accuracy numbers
findings.claim: The only result-reading utility prints the IDs of passed tasks; no committed script divides #passed by the benchmark total (500, 164, 1000, ...) to produce the reported accuracies (96.6%, 99.4%, etc.).
findings.concern: The headline accuracy values in Tables 1-4 cannot be regenerated by running a committed script; a reviewer must count passed task IDs and divide by the benchmark size by hand.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the script that converts the per-task `passed` flags into the reported accuracy (and over which trial directory it is run).
findings.reason: Quote at lines 10-23 of print_passed.py matches exactly; the three analysis scripts in the repo (print_passed.py, humaneval_print_and_eval.py, analyze_task_code_convergence.py) confirm no script divides #passed by a benchmark total to produce the reported percentages.

findings.title: RSR (Relative Success Rate) column reported in every table but never computed in repo
findings.quote: "RSR: Relative Success Rate = Accuracy gain over baseline normalized to full success."
findings.claim: Every results table reports an RSR (%) column, but a repo-wide search (`grep -rni 'rsr|relative'` over all .py, excluding submodules) finds no code that computes RSR.
findings.concern: The RSR values (e.g. 80.23, 94.04, 32.29) cannot be traced to any committed computation; they can only be re-derived if one already knows the formula and the baseline accuracies.
findings.resolution: Authors: provide the script computing RSR from EG-CFG accuracy and the baseline accuracy, including which baseline run each RSR uses.
findings.reason: Repo-wide grep for 'rsr' and 'relative' across all .py files returns no results, confirming no RSR computation script exists.

findings.title: Table 5 per-task runtime Mean ± SD has no computing/aggregation script
findings.quote: "Table 5: Per-task runtime statistics (in seconds) for each model and method on MBPP."
findings.claim: Per-task JSONs record `stats.duration`, but no committed script reads those durations across tasks to compute the Mean ± SD reported in Table 5 (123.23 ± 344.91, 271.37 ± 271.45, etc.).
findings.concern: The runtime comparison (a fairness argument central to the 'wall-clock time is the fair metric' claim) is not reproducible from a committed script.
findings.resolution: Authors: add the script that aggregates per-task `duration` into the Table 5 statistics, for both EG-CFG and the baselines.
findings.reason: Grep for 'duration', 'mean', 'std', and 'runtime' across all .py files finds no aggregation code; the three analysis scripts do not compute Mean ± SD from per-task durations.

findings.title: Ablation toggles exist but no driver reproduces Table 6 rows
findings.claim: The three Table 6 ablations map to togglable settings (γ=1 via `gammas`, `minimal_trace` in the README config table, and disabling beam search), but no committed config/driver fixes these to the exact ablation settings nor aggregates the four resulting accuracies.
findings.concern: Table 6 ablation numbers (58.2, 75.2, 76.4 ...) are not reproducible by running a committed, named configuration; the reader must reconstruct each ablation's config.
findings.resolution: Authors: please provide the exact configs (or a driver) used for the 'no beam search', 'γ=1', and 'minimal trace' ablation rows.
findings.reason: Config file at lines 1-9 matches the quote exactly; the single generic config sweeps all gammas together without isolating any specific Table 6 ablation row, confirming no dedicated ablation driver exists.

findings.title: Full per-task run outputs not shipped (only a 9-task analysis subset)
findings.claim: `trials/`, `results*/`, and `output/` are git-ignored, so none of the per-task result JSONs that underlie Tables 1-5 are in the repo; only `analysis/mbpp_analysis/` (9 tasks) is committed.
findings.concern: Because the headline numbers are produced by selecting, across ~192 (config × γ) attempts per task, the first that passes the test cases, the absence of the run logs means the exact selection that yielded each reported accuracy cannot be independently inspected without a full (and expensive) re-run.
findings.resolution: Authors: consider releasing the per-task solved-task JSONs (or the `solved_tasks/` caches) for at least one benchmark so the reported accuracy can be recomputed directly.
findings.reason: .gitignore lines 1-4 match the quote exactly, confirming that trials/, results*/, and output/ are all git-ignored and the per-task result JSONs are not committed.

findings.topic: evaluation protocol / selection on test labels
findings.title: Reported accuracy is best-of-~192 candidates selected by passing the evaluation test cases
findings.claim: For each task the framework sweeps a grid of decoding configs (paper: t∈{6 values}, d∈{4}, s=3, two prompt templates = 48 configs) × γ∈{0,0.5,1,3}, i.e. up to ~192 generations, and `solve_single_problem` accepts the first whose `passed` flag is True, where `passed` is computed by `run_tests(solution, test_cases_to_eval, ...)` against the *evaluation* test cases. The reported metric is therefore pass@(many candidates) with the selection oracle being the evaluation tests themselves, not pass@1.
findings.concern: Because the candidate that is reported is the first to pass the evaluation tests, the held-out test labels are used to choose which of ~192 generations to keep; this inflates accuracy relative to a single-shot or test-blind-selection protocol and is the mechanism behind the reported gains, so the headline SOTA numbers must be read as 'best of a large candidate budget chosen by the test cases', not single-attempt accuracy.
findings.resolution: Authors: report pass@1 (single fixed config, no test-based selection) alongside the best-of-grid number, and clarify in the tables that the reported accuracy uses test-case-passing as the cross-config selection criterion; state the exact candidate budget per benchmark.
findings.reason: Code at lines 588-591 matches the quote; line 616-618 shows `if solution_entry['passed']: break`, confirming the framework accepts the first passing candidate across the multi-config sweep using evaluation test cases as the selection oracle.

findings.title: On MBPP the execution-guidance tests are byte-identical to the evaluation tests
findings.claim: For MBPP, the tests fed into the prompt and executed to build the execution-guidance signal are `problem["test_list"]` (line 235), and the tests used for final scoring are `eval_problem["test_list"]` (line 495) — the same MBPP 3-test list. So on MBPP the model is steered, token by token, by the exact unit tests it is then graded on.
findings.concern: The whole method optimizes generation toward passing the supplied tests; when those tests are also the evaluation tests (MBPP, and the public tests used as the within-run stop signal generally), the headline MBPP numbers reflect fitting-to-the-eval-tests rather than generalization — the paper itself flags MBPP/MBPP-ET tests as the non-hidden case, so the concern is the magnitude, not concealment.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm that the MBPP accuracy is computed on the same 3 tests used for guidance, and (ideally) report MBPP performance on a held-out test set disjoint from the guidance tests, as already done for HumanEval/CodeContests via `eval_test_list`.
findings.reason: Line 235 shows `test_cases_to_prompt = problem['test_list']` (guidance tests) and line 495 shows `test_cases_to_eval = eval_problem['test_list']` for MBPP (vs. `eval_test_list` for HumanEval/CodeContests), confirming MBPP guidance and evaluation tests are the same field.
